risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Using the random number generator in the statistical package Stata (version 9), and after stratification by geographical block (the administrative unit above the village), one author (ED) randomly selected 30 of the 134 study villages to receive intervention A and 30 to receive intervention B." Comment: This describes a computer-generated random allocation process with stratification, which is a proper random sequence generation method.

Quote: "Using the random number generator in the statistical package Stata (version 9), and after stratification by geographical block ... one author (ED) randomly selected 30 of the 134 study villages to receive intervention A and 30 to receive intervention B. The 74 remaining villages were control villages" Comment: This describes random sequence generation and who performed the allocation, but it does not state any concealment mechanism (e.g., central allocation, sealed opaque envelopes, pharmacy-controlled assignment) preventing recruiters from foreseeing the next village assignment before enrollment. The paper also notes that "the allocation of villages to treatment or control was not blind," which is insufficient by itself to judge allocation concealment as low or high.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Naturally, given the context and the intervention, the allocation of villages to treatment or control was not blind." Comment: Participants and personnel could not be blinded to assignment because the intervention involved visibly different service delivery (immunisation camps and incentives) versus control, and the paper explicitly states allocation was not blind. Knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence immunisation uptake and related outcomes, so performance bias is high for the study overall.

Quote: "Both surveys were blind: interviewers did not know which villages belonged to which intervention (or control) group." Comment: The main outcomes were assessed by survey interviewers/field officers interviewing mothers and examining children for BCG scar, and these assessors were explicitly blinded to allocation. Although the primary outcome was largely mother self-report, the interviewers collecting and validating the data were blinded, and the study also used objective corroboration (BCG scar and logbooks). The quoted text directly supports blinded outcome assessment, so detection bias is low.

Quote: "The same households were surveyed again at the end point." and "The final sample in the household surveys included 2188 children aged 1-3 years at the end point from 2898 households..." Comment: The paper indicates follow-up of the same households and provides complete end-point sample sizes by arm, with no indication of substantial loss to follow-up or differential attrition. The reported analysis is intention-to-treat, and there is no evidence of high or imbalanced missing outcome data that would threaten the results.
